frame_label: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Mom, you won't believe it. This *one* cousin of Rohan's, Preeti... I mean, she's nice, but the amount of makeup she piles on! Itna foundation, full contouring and all. Honestly, she looked so different from her wedding photos, I almost didn't recognise her. People were whispering, saying 'sab makeup ka kamaal hai'. I mean, why hide your real face? It just looks so... unnatural, especially for a family function.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment critiques a woman for wearing 'too much makeup', implying deceptiveness ('hide your real face') and a lack of authenticity ('unnatural'). It aligns with the F13 core belief that visible effort to look attractive is 'unnatural' or 'hiding something', and references the India-specific matrimonial context of comparing looks from wedding photos.
original_frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
persona_text: You are Meera. Age 27. Married. A woman from a Brahmin family in Pune who married a man from a Kshatriya family in Rajasthan. The two families have different traditional dress norms for women. Your husband's family expects you to wear a saree and cover your head at family functions. Your own family never required this. You are on a phone call with your mother after a particularly difficult family function at your in-laws' house where your mother in law made a comment in front of everyone about how you were dressed. You are telling your mother what was said and how you felt and what your husband did and did not do and what you think is happening to your sense of yourself inside this marriage.
